portions.t: Brief summary/description of the presentation. Remember, strong slides are a support for your entire presentation, not a substitute.
Your presentation is a live experience carried by the presenter, so it’s important to visually represent your points without asking your audience to divide their attention too much.
Look for a narrative thread behind your presentation content and consider presenting your information beginning-to-end. Follow a situation to its consequences or a problem to its solution.
Weave the thread throughout your presentation. This will help it feel cohesive and make it easier to follow than a jumble of disjointed slides

This section is a good place to talk about the various causes of "the problem" or give some background on how many people it affects, how big the potential market is, or other compelling numbers. Make it interesting but keep it concise.
Remember, you want your audience focused on you, not trying to read a novel up on the screen.

Charts let your audience understand the numbers at a glance, so you can present a lot of information without breaking pace.
Pull out and highlight the most compelling data points.
This is where you first introduce your audience to the solution you provide to the problem(s) you’ve been talking about, so make it memorable.
Give an overview of your solution, highlighting the most important points.
How are you going to reach your targets?
Use images rather than trying to describe your subject.
Cluttered presentations are distracting and ineffective.
One consistent tone helps your presentation flow.

and discuss how your team is hitting them.

This is the place to highlight your biggest accomplishments to date.

Audiences respond well to stories. Look for a narrative thread behind your presentation content and consider presenting your information beginning-to-end. Follow a situation to its consequences or a problem to its solution.
Weave the thread throughout your presentation. This will help it feel cohesive and make it easier to follow than a jumble of disjointed slides.
Tailor your slideshow to your audience’s needs. Consider their level of familiarity with the subject matter, industry jargon, and background.
If you’re using language they're likely to be unfamiliar with, offer definitions so they don’t feel lost.
You have a lot of knowledge to share. Decide which points you most want your audience to remember after they leave.
Write your presentation with those points in mind, so your most important message is clear and memorable.
